current_fact.fact_text: On March 7, 2006, Sweden’s future NATO partner was far from the news, but in 2006 Varanasi, bombs shattered a centuries‑old rhythm along the Ganges. Devotees went from evening prayers to emergency chaos in seconds, revealing how sacred routines and sudden violence can occupy the same narrow alleyways.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

current_fact.match_reason: The tension between sacred city life and bombing in Varanasi pairs with meditative reggae and dub, offering a sonic space to process shock and seek calm.

queue.fact_text: On March 7, 2007, while ballots and debates reshaped the British constitution, another kind of reform was happening in sound: Auto‑Tune was cementing itself as a global studio staple. Within a few years, the subtle pitch‑fixing tool would become an aesthetic choice, turning human voices into shimmering, alien instruments.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description: In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A turning point in how we think about perfection and the human voice is matched with the dedicated episode that unpacks this very transformation in music.

queue.fact_text: On March 7, 2024, armorer Hannah Gutierrez-Reed was found guilty of involuntary manslaughter in the death of cinematographer Halyna Hutchins on the set of the film "Rust." It was the first time in U.S. history that someone was criminally convicted for a death caused by a prop gun on a movie set, exposing how illusions of safety can fail behind the camera.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description: In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A tragic accident on a film set and its legal reckoning align with a reflective episode that includes a track literally titled “This Is a Film,” inviting contemplation about risk, art, and hindsight.

queue.fact_text: On March 7, 1965 in the U.S. and across the world, the civil rights movement was reshaping how societies understood power — and decades later, women would claim more of that power in music and beyond. Each baton, bullet, and shouted demand laid groundwork for future generations to stand at microphones instead of barricades.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

queue.match_reason: The civil rights era’s push for equality connects to an episode celebrating female MCs claiming space and voice in hip-hop, echoing the long arc of liberation.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1902 at Tweebosch, 1814 at Craonne, and 1951 in Korea, different empires and alliances were all testing the limits of force. Taken together, March 7 reads like a recurring footnote in military history — a reminder that the calendar can quietly align distant battles into an accidental constellation of conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A meta‑reflection on multiple wars and battles fits with Rage Against the Machine’s explicitly anti‑war, anti‑imperialist energy, closing the day on a note of questioning power itself.

published.fact_text: In 1876, Alexander Graham Bell was granted a patent for a strange device he called the “telephone,” which turned electrical signals into recognizable human speech. Many of his contemporaries saw it as a novelty rather than a revolution, yet within just a few decades, it rewired how the planet thought about distance and connection.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: An episode about our dependency on phones is a perfect mirror to Bell’s first leap in telecommunication, tracing the line from the earliest telephone to the hyperconnected present.

published.fact_text: On March 7, 1941, German U-boat commander Günther Prien and the crew of U-47 disappeared without a trace in the North Atlantic. Their submarine, once famed for slipping into Scapa Flow and sinking a British battleship, became its own unsolved wartime mystery beneath the waves.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A sea-bound German submarine vanishing in wartime is contrasted with a peaceful modern voyage with Germans under open skies, turning the ocean from battlefield into shared playground.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1986, deep-sea divers from the USS Preserver located the crew cabin of the Space Shuttle Challenger on the ocean floor. The find brought painful closure to one of spaceflight’s darkest moments, where a journey meant to pierce the sky ended in fragments scattered across the sea.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Both stories involve divers descending into cold, deep water to meet the aftermath of human ambition — one in search of whales, the other of a lost spacecraft.

published.fact_text: On March 7, 2024, Sweden formally joined NATO, becoming the alliance’s 32nd member after nearly two centuries of military non-alignment. The shift underscored how rapidly security landscapes can change when geography, history, and contemporary threats collide.

episode.title: Morning Drops #10 - The One With Many Names
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the glamorous world of Ghost, the Swedish band that turned rock into ritual. From masked clergy to shifting identities, diving into the music and myth of one of the most theatrical acts of our time.

published.match_reason: A major geopolitical turn by Sweden pairs naturally with a deep dive into one of Sweden’s most theatrical bands, linking national identity, ritual, and reinvention.

published.fact_text: On March 7, 1931, Finland inaugurated its Parliament House in Helsinki, a monumental granite structure blending classical columns with stark Nordic simplicity. The building symbolized a young nation’s confidence in democracy and architecture’s power to shape civic imagination.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

published.match_reason: A northern capital’s parliament rising in stone matches an episode about northern landscapes and what grounds us, echoing themes of place, identity, and quiet resilience.

published.fact_text: During the Second Boer War on this day in 1902, Boer forces under Koos de la Rey defeated a much larger British column at the Battle of Tweebosch. The surprise victory, achieved with mobile commandos on the open veld, shocked the British and hastened the push toward peace negotiations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A guerrilla battle on open African plains resonates with Tuareg desert blues, where music tells stories of riders, resistance, and survival in wide, harsh landscapes.

published.fact_text: In 1814, Napoleon I won the Battle of Craonne in France, hurling his exhausted army against Russian and Prussian forces along a snow-dusted plateau. It was a costly, narrow victory, symbolizing a twilight moment: the tactical brilliance was still there, but the empire itself was beginning to crumble.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description: In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

published.match_reason: A late, rain-soaked victory in a fading era pairs with an episode about endings, storms, and the quiet hope of moving into a new chapter.

published.fact_text: On March 7, 1799, Napoleon captured the fortified port of Jaffa in Palestine during his Middle Eastern campaign, and his troops proceeded to massacre more than 2,000 surrendered Albanian prisoners. The brutal episode still shadows his legend, challenging the image of the enlightened military genius.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A violent imperial expedition through the Eastern Mediterranean contrasts with an episode about travel as awareness, reframing journeys from conquest to reflection.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1951, United Nations forces led by General Matthew Ridgway launched Operation Ripper in the Korean War, a massive offensive aimed at retaking Seoul from Chinese and North Korean troops. It showed how, even under the UN flag, war could still look like a grinding, conventional battle of artillery and infantry.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A UN-led offensive in a polarized war pairs with an episode centered on overtly political music, amplifying themes of conflict, power, and resistance.

published.fact_text: On March 7, 1951, Iranian Prime Minister Ali Razmara was assassinated outside a mosque in Tehran by Khalil Tahmasebi, a member of the militant group Fada'iyan-e Islam. His death removed a key opponent of nationalizing Iran’s oil, nudging the country toward the dramatic Mosaddegh era and future upheavals.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: A pivotal Iranian assassination linked to oil and geopolitics matches an eclectic, globally-minded mix that crosses borders and roots, echoing the region’s complexity.

published.fact_text: On March 7, 1965, about 600 civil rights marchers attempted to walk from Selma to Montgomery, Alabama, and were savagely attacked by state troopers and local police on the Edmund Pettus Bridge. The day became known as “Bloody Sunday” and, broadcast on television, helped galvanize support for the U.S. Voting Rights Act.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: A historic march for civil rights sits alongside an episode of conscious rap about identity and resistance, connecting past struggles to contemporary voices.

published.fact_text: Also on March 7, 1965, Aeroflot Flight 542 broke apart mid‑air over the Yermakovsky District in Siberia, killing all 31 people aboard. Investigators later pointed to structural failure in severe turbulence, a reminder that even routine flights can cross invisible thresholds of stress and cold.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden, catastrophic structural failure in flight is paired with an episode themed around jolts and shocks, reflecting the abrupt rupture in an otherwise ordinary journey.

published.fact_text: On March 7, 1951, Northwest Orient Airlines Flight 307 crashed into a neighborhood in Lynnhurst, Minneapolis, killing 15 people. The aircraft struck a flagpole and then a house during its approach, turning a quiet residential evening into a scene of fire and investigation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A fall from the sky into a lake-adjacent neighborhood contrasts with an episode about gently submerging and floating, turning a violent descent into a meditation on depth and calm.

published.fact_text: On March 7, 2007, Garuda Indonesia Flight 200 overran the runway at Yogyakarta’s Adisutjipto Airport and burst into flames, killing 21 people. Many passengers survived by forcing open emergency exits and escaping through burning fuel, a chaotic scene where seconds meant everything.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: An intense, fiery crash at the end of a rushed descent is set against an episode dedicated to slowing down, offering a sonic antidote to speed and disaster.

published.fact_text: On March 7, 2006, the militant group Lashkar‑e‑Taiba orchestrated a series of bomb blasts in Varanasi, India, targeting a crowded Hindu temple and a railway station. The attacks struck one of the world’s oldest continuously inhabited cities, where layers of spirituality and daily life sit impossibly close together.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and Almudena from Madrid. Exploring the roots and evolution of a tradition that is emotionally raw and culturally rich.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: A terrorist attack on a sacred, music-filled city is contrasted with an episode exploring another deeply spiritual musical tradition, honoring how culture and sound outlast violence.

published.fact_text: On March 7, 1989, Iran and the United Kingdom broke diplomatic relations amid uproar over Salman Rushdie’s novel “The Satanic Verses.” A work of fiction triggered real-world fatwas, protests, and book burnings, revealing how stories can become geopolitical flashpoints.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description: In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

published.match_reason: A novel that sparked global controversy aligns with an episode about a boundary‑pushing lyricist, both showing how words and art can ignite political storms.

published.fact_text: On this day in 2009, the Real Irish Republican Army attacked Massereene Barracks in Northern Ireland, killing two British soldiers and injuring four others. It was the first fatal attack on British troops in the region since the end of the Troubles, a grim reminder that some conflicts smolder long after the headlines move on.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

published.match_reason: A post‑conflict attack in the UK is paired with an episode about a British industrial city’s sound, highlighting how places carry both grit and creativity in their identity.

published.fact_text: On March 7, 2007, the British House of Commons voted in favor of turning the unelected House of Lords into a 100% elected chamber. Though the reform stalled, the vote was a striking moment when tradition, democracy, and constitutional engineering collided under televised lights.

episode.title: Morning Drops #50 - Postcards from Earth-London
episode.description:
Postcards from Earth-London is a Morning Drops walk through the city Gaia has called home for the past ten years, a place made of sound, memory, and late-night streets.
Four tracks guide the journey: from iconic landmarks to quieter corners, from romance to reality checks, and back to the reason the city still feels alive: music.

published.match_reason: A bold attempt to rewrite Britain’s upper chamber fits with an ode to London itself, the city where these debates play out in real time.

published.fact_text: On March 7, 2021, a series of powerful explosions ripped through a military barracks complex in Bata, Equatorial Guinea, killing at least 108 people and injuring hundreds more. Authorities blamed poorly stored explosives and a nearby fire, exposing how invisible risks can build for years in the heart of a city.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: A tragedy in an African coastal city is paired with an episode celebrating African musical resilience, honoring how rhythm and culture persist amid instability.

published.fact_text: On March 7, 2006, Sweden’s future NATO partner was far from the news, but in 2006 Varanasi, bombs shattered a centuries‑old rhythm along the Ganges. Devotees went from evening prayers to emergency chaos in seconds, revealing how sacred routines and sudden violence can occupy the same narrow alleyways.

episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.

King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown
